title: Musakhan
subtitle: Palestinian roasted chicken with sumac, onions, and pitta
excerpt: Chicken pieces roasted in olive oil and heavily seasoned with sumac and allspice, placed on a bed of slow-cooked caramelised onions and served on pitta bread that has soaked up all the cooking juices. The sumac is applied in quantities that would seem excessive for any other dish — here it is the main flavour, tart and fruity, against the sweet onion and rich chicken fat.
sourceNotes: Musakhan is widely considered the Palestinian national dish. It is documented in Sami Tamimi and Yotam Ottolenghi's 'Jerusalem' (2012) and in Palestinian cookbooks as a celebration and harvest dish, traditionally made in autumn when the new olive oil is pressed. The pitta beneath the chicken absorbs the cooking juices and is considered as important as the chicken itself.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cook the onions up to 2 days ahead. Reheat with the chicken juices before assembling.

content.text: The onions are the foundation of musakhan. They need to cook slowly in olive oil for at least 40 minutes — long enough to become sweet, golden-brown, and jammy. The sumac is added to the onions at the end of their cooking and also rubbed directly onto the chicken, so the tartness permeates the whole dish. Use good olive oil; this is one dish where the flavour of the oil is important, not just its function.

The pitta goes in at the end: laid flat on the serving platter, topped with the cooked onions, then the roasted chicken, and all the juices from the roasting pan poured over. The pitta should be saturated with the juice. This is not elegant plating — it is comfort food designed to be eaten by hand, with each person tearing off a piece of sumac-soaked bread along with the chicken.

Heat 4 tablespoons of the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a large heavy pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook, stirring occasionally, for 40 to 45 minutes until very soft and deep golden. Season with salt. Stir in 2 tablespoons of the {{sumac}} and the {{cinnamon-ground}}, cook for 1 minute, and remove from the heat.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Mix the remaining {{sumac}}, {{allspice}}, salt, and remaining {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} to a paste. Rub all over the {{chicken-thigh}} pieces. Place in a roasting pan and roast for 35 to 40 minutes until the skin is dark and the juices run clear.

Lay the {{pitta-bread}} flat on a large serving platter. Spoon the caramelised onions over the pitta. Place the roasted chicken pieces on top. Pour all the juices from the roasting pan over everything. Top with toasted {{pine-nuts}} if using. Serve immediately, letting everyone tear into the soaked bread and chicken.

items.fix: Musakhan uses a large quantity of sumac — this is not a dish where you can moderate it. 4 tablespoons is the right amount. Taste the onions before adding more: they should be distinctly tart

items.cause: Too much liquid or heat too low
items.fix: The onions release a lot of water at first. Keep the heat at medium-high and stir more frequently in the second half of cooking. Do not cover

items.symptom: Pitta is soggy and falling apart
items.fix: The pitta is meant to absorb all the juices. If it bothers you, use a day-old pitta that is slightly dry, which will absorb without disintegrating completely

items.cause: Too much oil in the rub or oven not hot enough
items.fix: The rub should be a thin coating, not a thick paste. Pat the chicken dry before rubbing. The last 5 minutes under the grill crisps the skin effectively

content.text: Musakhan is the Palestinian national dish, traditionally made in October at the olive harvest when the new oil is pressed. In Palestinian villages the dish was historically brought to the communal oven (the taboon) in large trays, with the whole family contributing to the preparation and the meal eaten communally from a shared platter. The flavour of fresh-pressed olive oil in the onions and on the chicken is considered essential to the dish at its best. Musakhan has gained wider recognition through Palestinian diaspora cooking and through Sami Tamimi's work, which placed it on the map for non-Arab audiences. The dish is a study in simplicity: four main ingredients, direct technique, generous seasoning.
